stories.title: From NAIOP to CREDA: New Name, Expanded Scope, Same Mission
stories.excerpt: The rebranding of NAIOP to the Commercial Real Estate Development Association didn’t occur overnight—the association has worked for years on crafting its new identity. Nor is it the first time the organization has undergone a name change; in fact, President and CEO Marc Selvitelli told Connect CRE, the new name is its fifth since its establishment as the National Association of Industrial Parks in 1967. Selvitelli sat down with Connect CRE to discuss the rebranding and the future direction of what is now the Commercial Real Estate Development Association. Q: Why was now the proper time to rebrand as the Commercial Real Estate Development Association?   A: The simple answer is that our membership has changed. I’ve been with the organization since 2006 and can tell you that when I came on, the vast majority of our members were office developers. Next were industrial, and after that, it was a small mishmash of other property types. Now, the number one asset class for our membership is industrial, the second is mixed-use, the third is multifamily, the fourth is data centers, and office is fifth. This reflects a real change in our members’ primary focuses. Part of it is that there’s been a change in how commercial real estate companies operate. They used to just be primarily in one asset class. Office developers only did office. But as time went by, whether through mergers and acquisitions or just sound risk management, many developers began working in other asset classes too, providing a buffer. We can use the current environment as a good example, where office right now is a little soft, but multifamily has done well. And if you do both of those, your balance sheet isn’t nearly as impacted.   Q: In addition to the fact that many developers now encompass multiple property types, what are some other ways that development has evolved? And how and why was it important for the Commercial Real Estate Developmen
bullets: NAIOP rebranded to CREDA; membership now 40% industrial, 30% mixed-use, 20% multifamily.
Developers diversifying across asset classes for risk management as office softens.
Multifamily portfolio balance shields firms from single-asset-class cyclicality.
stories.summary: NAIOP's rebrand to Commercial Real Estate Development Association reflects fundamental shifts in member strategy: industrial now leads (44% of portfolio), multifamily ranks third, and office has fallen to fifth. The organization's CEO noted developers increasingly operate across multiple asset classes—a risk mitigation strategy where multifamily strength offsets office weakness. This mirrors broader industry consolidation and portfolio diversification trends.
stories.impact: Market consolidation favors larger, diversified firms with multifamily exposure; smaller, single-asset specialists face competitive pressure. Multifamily developers should expect continued cross-sector competition for capital and talent from industrial/data-center platforms.

stories.title: If New York’s Rent Guidelines Board Ignores Its Own Data, Why Do We Need It?
stories.excerpt: The New York City Rent Guidelines Board’s decision to impose the first-ever two-year freeze on rent-stabilized apartments raises a simple question: Why commission months of economic research if you’re going to ignore it? Or put another way: If the Rent Guidelines Board (RGB) ignores its own data, why do we need the board? That is not a rhetorical question. It goes to the heart of why the RGB was created. The board’s statutory responsibility is straightforward: Establish rent adjustments that balance tenant affordability with the economic viability of maintaining nearly 1 million rent-stabilized apartments. The idea is simple. Collect the data, evaluate the facts, and make a decision based on the evidence. Ben Tapper. Photo: Courtesy of Lee & Associates This year, however, the RGB ignored the very information it gathered to help make that decision. The RGB’s own 2026 price index of operating costs found that operating costs for rent-stabilized buildings increased 5.3 percent over the past year. Insurance costs rose 10.5 percent, fuel increased 11 percent, maintenance climbed 6 percent, and property taxes continued to rise. Its own net revenue projection concluded that rents would have needed to increase approximately 3.4 to 4.5 percent simply to maintain owners’ existing net operating income — not increase profits, merely keep pace with rising expenses. Those findings should have established the lower boundary of any economically rational rent adjustment. Instead, the RGB adopted a 0 percent increase. Reasonable people can disagree about what the increase should have been. That’s why the board exists. But it’s hard to understand how the answer became zero after the board’s own research pointed in a completely different direction. That disconnect became even more apparent during the public discussion. Maksim Wynn, the RGB member appointed to represent property owners, argued that freezing rents was actually doing own
bullets: NYC RGB imposed first-ever two-year rent freeze on stabilized units despite 5.3% operating-cost inflation.
Insurance costs up 10.5%, fuel 11%, maintenance 6%—data contradicts freeze decision.
Board ignored its own economic research; legitimacy of rent-setting mechanism questioned.
stories.summary: New York City's Rent Guidelines Board froze rents on 1 million rent-stabilized apartments for two years despite its own price index showing 5.3% operating-cost growth (insurance +10.5%, fuel +11%, maintenance +6%). The freeze prioritizes tenant affordability over owner viability, directly contradicting the board's statutory mandate to balance both. The disconnect between data and policy raises questions about the board's continued relevance.
stories.impact: Rent-stabilized building economics deteriorate sharply in NYC, threatening deferred maintenance and eventual disinvestment. For multifamily developers, this eliminates new stabilized-unit construction economics and pressures market-rate yields upward as owners shift capital; likely accelerates condo conversion and pushes new supply toward luxury/market-rate segments.

stories.title: Emory Healthcare Expanding Northlake Mall Presence
stories.excerpt: Emory Healthcare is expanding its footprint at Northlake Mall. Since 2019, Emory Healthcare has leased approximately 274,000 square feet at the mall. The new lease will more than double that occupancy. Other than Emory Healthcare, the mall, which opened in 1971, is mostly vacant. The increased presence follows an ownership change for the property. A non-profit, The Centurian Foundation, paid $95 million for the Northlake Mall. Due to its non-profit status, up to $225 million in bonds could be issued to finance the costs of acquisition, demolition, construction, expansion, renovation, repair, build-out and/or equipping of existing facilities at the mall site. Emory Healthcare currently houses a variety of administrative and clinical operations at Northlake Mall. In recent years, the site has also grown to include a primary care center, which opened in 2025, and a child care center operated in partnership with Primrose School, which opened in 2024. The post Emory Healthcare Expanding Northlake Mall Presence appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Emory Healthcare expanding Northlake Mall (Atlanta) from 274k to 550k+ sq ft.
Centurian Foundation (nonprofit) acquired mall for $95M; $225M bond capacity unlocked.
Healthcare anchor driving adaptive reuse; clinical + administrative + childcare co-location model.
stories.summary: Emory Healthcare is more than doubling its footprint at Atlanta's Northlake Mall, expanding from 274,000 to 550,000+ square feet. The property changed hands to nonprofit Centurian Foundation, which now can access $225 million in tax-exempt financing for redevelopment. Emory's presence—spanning administrative offices, primary care, and childcare—signals a master-planned healthcare campus anchoring an otherwise vacant suburban mall.
stories.impact: Northlake Mall's transformation from retail to healthcare-led mixed-use creates localized employment cluster and medical-services demand. For nearby multifamily developers in North Atlanta, this anchors residential demand for healthcare workers, caregivers, and service employees; suggests premium pricing opportunity for workforce/service housing near the mall site.

stories.title: Greystone Real Estate Capital Closes $137M Affordable Housing Development Fund
stories.excerpt: Stephen Rosenberg’s Greystone Real Estate Capital continues its equity push into U.S. affordable housing. Commercial Observer can first report that Greystone Real Estate Capital closed its second affordable housing fund — Greystone Affordable Housing Fund II — securing $137 million of Low-Income Housing Tax Credit (LIHTC) financing from several investors to support the development and preservation of nearly 2,000 affordable housing units across 20 properties in nine states. Fund II follows the earlier launch of Greystone Affordable Housing Fund I, which closed in August 2025 with approximately $105 million of institutional investor equity. This fund has since invested into the development and preservation of almost 1,000 affordable housing units in 11 U.S. real estate projects. The closing of Fund II utilized capital from eight institutional LIHTC investors, whose names could not be released, but three of them are repeat investors from Fund I. All total, Greystone has raised $240 million of new investor capital for national affordable housing projects in less than 12 months. “The closing of Fund II reflects both the market’s confidence in our affordable housing platform and Greystone’s longstanding commitment to creating and preserving affordable housing nationwide,” said Rosenberg, CEO of Greystone, in a statement. Todd Jones, chief investment officer at Greystone Real Estate Capital, told CO via email that the firm’s impetus to create the funds came from the ongoing national shortage of affordable housing and “the growing institutional investor demand for high-quality impact investments,” which can benefit from tax incentives in the Community Reinvestment Act. “Institutional investors are increasingly seeking investments that generate meaningful social impact alongside stable, long-term risk adjusted returns,” said Jones. The new equity will be split between 60 percent new construction and 40 percent rehabilitation of affordable housing projects, with
bullets: Greystone raised $137M in LIHTC Fund II; supports 2,000 units across 20 properties, 9 states.
Fund I ($105M, Aug 2025) already deployed to 1,000 units; total $240M committed.
Repeat institutional LIHTC investors signal sustained appetite for affordable preservation/development.
stories.summary: Greystone Real Estate Capital closed its second Low-Income Housing Tax Credit fund at $137 million, targeting development and preservation of nearly 2,000 affordable units across 20 properties in nine states. Combined with Fund I ($105 million, closed August 2025), Greystone has now raised $240 million of institutional LIHTC capital. Three repeat institutional investors participated in Fund II, signaling consistent capital availability.
stories.impact: Stable LIHTC capital markets support affordable preservation and new supply nationally. For multifamily developers targeting mixed-income or workforce housing, this demonstrates viable equity/finance pathways and signals competition from institutional affordable players; developers should track LIHTC fund sizes and investor appetite as bellwethers of capital availability.

stories.title: Beachwold Closes on $176.6M Financing for 1,395-Bed UCF Student Housing Venture
stories.excerpt: Beachwold Residential obtained a $176.6 million construction loan to finance the development of Place at Alafaya Phase I & II. The to-be-built, four-building, four-story, Class A, 484-unit, 1,395-bed student housing property will be located at the University of Central Florida (UCF) in Orlando, Florida. Affinius Capital originated the loan. The financing was arranged by Mona Carlton, Elliott Throne, and Josh Odessky of JLL. Phase I will offer a 284-unit, 809-bed, two-building, four-story property that will deliver prior to the 2028/2029 academic year. Phase II will consist of a 200-unit, 586-bed property with 534 parking spaces, to be delivered prior to the 2029/2030 academic year.  A three-story, 16,000-square-foot standalone clubhouse will serve as the heart of the property, featuring a boutique coffee bar, social and study lounges, gaming and theater areas, and a top-floor fitness and wellness center complete with sauna and cold plunge. Onsite amenities will include lagoon-style pools with cabanas, multiple courtyards, pickleball courts, a half-court basketball area, and a direct shuttle service to the UCF campus. Stay ahead of the multifamily market at Connect Apartments. Gain firsthand insights from industry leaders on capital markets, development, operations, financing, and investment trends while expanding your network with the people making deals happen. See you on September 22! The post Beachwold Closes on $176.6M Financing for 1,395-Bed UCF Student Housing Venture appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Beachwold secured $176.6M construction loan for 484-unit (1,395-bed) UCF student housing.
Phase I: 284 units (809 beds) delivered 2028/29; Phase II: 200 units (586 beds) 2029/30.
Affinius Capital originated; high-amenity clubhouse and resort-style pool/recreation model.
stories.summary: Beachwold Residential obtained $176.6 million construction financing from Affinius Capital for Place at Alafaya, a 484-unit (1,395-bed) Class A student housing project at UCF in Orlando. Phase I (284 units, 809 beds) delivers 2028/29; Phase II (200 units, 586 beds) 2029/30. The property features a 16,000-sq-ft clubhouse, lagoon pools, multiple courtyards, and campus shuttle—high-amenity positioning for premium student rents.
stories.impact: Strong student housing construction activity near UCF reflects continued demand for Class A beds and premium amenity-driven rents in the submarket. For Orlando-area multifamily developers, this signals tight supply of new, high-end beds; indicates rental rate growth runway and competition for campus-proximate land; also shows lender appetite for construction on student assets.

stories.title: CentrePoint Acquires 3 Denver-Area Retail Centers
stories.excerpt: Cushman & Wakefield announced today that the real estate services firm has arranged the $30.3 million sale of a three-property retail portfolio, including Cottonwood Square in Parker, Bristol Pointe in Aurora and Foothill Green in Littleton. The portfolio totals over 100,000 square feet of retail space.Jon Hendrickson and Aaron Johnson of Cushman & Wakefield represented the seller, Western Centers, Inc. The property was acquired by CentrePoint Properties. Craig Branton and Mickey Moran of Greystone, in partnership with Cushman & Wakefield’s Denver-based debt team, arranged the acquisition financing for all three properties.Cottonwood Square is a 56,000-square-foot neighborhood center located just north of E-470 and Parker Road, and adjacent to King Soopers. Bristol Pointe, a 26,000-square-foot center, features a service- and restaurant-driven tenant roster and has local employment drivers stemming from proximity to Buckley Space Force Base. Foothill Green is 28,000 square feet and is the unanchored neighborhood center. Stay ahead of the multifamily market at Connect Apartments. Gain firsthand insights from industry leaders on capital markets, development, operations, financing, and investment trends while expanding your network with the people making deals happen. See you on September 22! The post CentrePoint Acquires 3 Denver-Area Retail Centers appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: CentrePoint acquired 3 Denver-area retail centers for $30.3M (100k+ sq ft total).
Portfolio includes Cottonwood Square (56k sf), Bristol Pointe (26k sf), Foothill Green (28k sf).
Bristol Pointe near Buckley Space Force Base; Greystone arranged debt.
stories.summary: CentrePoint Properties acquired a three-property retail portfolio totaling 100,000+ square feet in the Denver metro (Parker, Aurora, Littleton) for $30.3 million. Cottonwood Square (56k sf) is anchored by King Soopers; Bristol Pointe (26k sf) is service/restaurant-driven near Buckley Space Force Base; Foothill Green (28k sf) is an unanchored neighborhood center. Greystone arranged acquisition financing.
stories.impact: Retail portfolio consolidation in Denver suburbs indicates investor focus on stable, food/service-anchored neighborhood centers with military/employment anchors. Limited direct multifamily relevance; retail-to-multifamily conversion feasibility low given remaining retail productivity.

stories.title: Flournoy Properties Lands $46.5M Refi on Leander Rental Asset
stories.excerpt: Flournoy Properties closed on a $46.5 million refinancing of the River Junction apartments in Leander. The project, completed in 2024, has 329 units. Bridge Investment Group provided the loan. The Walker & Dunlop Capital Markets Real Estate Finance team, led by Patrick Short and Clay Colvill, arranged the floating-rate, interest-only bridge loan. Besides the 329 units, the garden-style multifamily community includes 17 townhomes across 15 residential buildings with one-, two-, and three-bedroom floor plans. Community amenities include a pool, fitness center, clubhouse, coworking space, pet park, BBQ areas, and detached garages and carports. The property, at 116 Talon Grasp Trail, benefits from accelerating occupancy as employment growth, led by the semiconductor industry and data center development, continues to drive demand. Walker & Dunlop’s Patrick Short added, “River Junction is well-positioned to capitalize on the continued growth of North Austin, where strong employment fundamentals and renter demand are supporting new Class A communities.” Stay ahead of the multifamily market at Connect Apartments. Gain firsthand insights from industry leaders on capital markets, development, operations, financing, and investment trends while expanding your network with the people making deals happen. See you on September 22! The post Flournoy Properties Lands $46.5M Refi on Leander Rental Asset appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Flournoy Properties closed $46.5M refi on 329-unit River Junction (Leander, TX), completed 2024.
Bridge Investment Group provided floating-rate, interest-only bridge loan via Walker & Dunlop.
North Austin employment growth (semiconductors, data centers) driving occupancy acceleration.
stories.summary: Flournoy Properties refinanced the recently completed (2024) River Junction apartments in Leander, north of Austin, via a $46.5 million floating-rate bridge loan from Bridge Investment Group arranged by Walker & Dunlop. The 329-unit garden-style community is experiencing accelerating occupancy driven by semiconductor and data center employment growth in North Austin. Amenities include pool, fitness, coworking, and pet facilities.
stories.impact: North Austin multifamily showing strong occupancy momentum tied to semiconductor/data-center employment. For developers in the Austin metro, this validates continued demand fundamentals around Leander, North Austin; suggests rent growth runway supports refinancing and development; indicates investor confidence in regional employment trajectory supporting Class A product.

stories.title: Citi Prices Largest Single Bank-Contributed, Multi-Only CMBS Conduit Deal Since GFC
stories.excerpt: Dog days of summer? Not for Citigroup, which just priced a record-setting deal. The bank this week priced the largest multifamily-only conduit commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS) transaction originated by a single bank since the Global Financial Crisis. The transaction, Citigroup Commercial Mortgage Trust 2026-MFAM1, is an all-multifamily conduit deal comprising 27 five-year, interest-only loans on 27 properties. It has an aggregate principal balance of $816.9 million, and 100 percent of the loans were contributed by Citi Real Estate Funding . In addition to the deal being the biggest single bank-originated multifamily-only conduit deal since the GFC, according to Trepp data, it also seemingly represents the largest loan contribution in a conduit deal by a single bank post-Covid. In 2016, the GSMS 2016-GS4 transaction was a single bank weighing in at a little over $1.2 billion. Further back, J.P. Morgan was the single mortgage loan seller in two conduit deals it issued in 2011: the $1.5 billion JPMCC 2011-C3 and $1.47 billion JPMCC 2011-C4 transactions. The Citigroup Commercial Mortgage Trust 2026-MFAM1 AAA bonds priced at swaps plus 80 basis points (bps) earlier this week, 8 bps tighter than the last cash-flow AAA tranche priced on the JPMF1 Multifamily Mortgage Trust 2026-FX1 transaction, which priced in May. In the latter deal, which was also 100 percent multifamily, MF1 Capital was the mortgage seller for 100 percent of the loans, which totaled $734 million. Sources familiar with the Citi execution said the bank saw a void — and appetite — in the market for medium- to high-leverage loans on high-quality multifamily product, and leaned in. The bank faced some inventory risk with the pool but fully committed to the program, holding terms on every loan’s execution, and everything went swimmingly as a result. According to Fitch Ratings, the Citi deal’s average Fitch loan-to-value (LTV) of 123.4 percent is higher than five-year multiborrower averages in both
bullets: Citigroup priced $816.9M multifamily-only CMBS conduit (27 properties, 27 loans), largest since GFC.
100% of loans from Citi Real Estate Funding; five-year, interest-only structure.
Single-bank origination at scale signals strong multifamily debt capital availability.
stories.summary: Citigroup priced CCMT 2026-MFAM1, a $816.9 million multifamily-only CMBS conduit comprising 27 five-year, interest-only loans on 27 properties—the largest single-bank-originated multifamily conduit since the Global Financial Crisis. All loans were originated by Citi Real Estate Funding. The deal represents the largest post-COVID single-bank contribution to a conduit transaction.
stories.impact: CMBS market strength and multifamily-focused investor appetite signal robust debt capital availability for stabilized assets. For developers and acquisitions players, this indicates healthy refinancing and sale-leaseback liquidity; lower cost of capital for performing multifamily portfolios; suggests cap rates remain compressed and pricing remains favorable for stabilized deals.

stories.title: South Florida Retail Markets Show Sub-5% Vacancy
stories.excerpt: Despite oft-repeated fears of retail’s pandemic-drive demise, South Florida’s retail sector is enjoying a combination of low vacancy rates, resilient rental rates and robust leasing and investment sales. That’s according to market reports released Thursday by commercial real estate brokerage Colliers. In Miami-Dade County, the retail market experienced net absorption of 378,795 square feet, driving the retail vacancy rate down to 3 percent in the second quarter of 2026, the lowest among the three South Florida counties. Average asking rents in Miami-Dade County rose to $42.50 per square foot per year, up 2.6 percent from the first quarter. More than 1.3 million square feet of retail space are under construction, Colliers reported. Floor & Decor Outlets of America signed the largest lease of the quarter with 55,146 square feet at Park Plaza Shoppes in Kendall. Planet Fitness followed with a 26,207-square-foot deal at 1501 Biscayne Boulevard in Miami. The biggest sale was a $210 million purchase at Miami WorldCenter by a joint venture involving Falcone Group, The Davis Companies and Jamestown. In Broward County, net absorption in the second quarter was a negative 102,436 square feet, causing the retail vacancy rate to increase slightly to 4.3 percent in the second quarter, up from 4.2 percent in the first quarter. Average asking rents in Broward fell to $27.67 per square foot, a 3.4 percent decline from the second quarter of 2025. Wayfair signed Broward County’s largest lease with a new 94,000-square-foot deal at Galleria Mall in Fort Lauderdale, followed by Apogee Lauderdale taking 29,309 square feet at the International Swimming Hall of Fame. In Palm Beach County, net absorption totaled 148,769 square feet during the second quarter, nudging the retail vacancy rate down to 4 percent. Average asking rents increased to $30.14 per square foot, an annual gain of 3.4 percent. Premier Indoor Pickleball led the quarter for Palm Beach County from the tenant side by commi
bullets: Miami-Dade retail vacancy fell to 3% (Q2 2026), lowest of three South Florida counties.
Average asking rents rose to $42.50/sf/year (+2.6% QoQ); 1.3M sf under construction.
Largest lease: Floor & Decor 55k sf; robust leasing and investment sales activity.
stories.summary: South Florida's retail sector is thriving with sub-5% vacancy rates and resilient rents across Miami-Dade, Broward, and Palm Beach counties. Miami-Dade County achieved 3% vacancy in Q2 2026, the tightest among the three, with average rents at $42.50/sf/year (up 2.6% QoQ). More than 1.3 million square feet of retail space are under construction; Floor & Decor leased 55,146 sf in Kendall, the quarter's largest deal.
stories.impact: Strong retail fundamentals in South Florida provide minimal conversion or development opportunity for multifamily; indicates healthy retail corridor anchors that support mixed-use and neighborhood multifamily demand. Developers should view retail strength as a positive sign for foot traffic and service-employment clustering near multifamily sites in urban/mixed-use submarkets.

stories.title: Prologis Beats Expectations With Record Leasing and Data Center Growth
stories.excerpt: The world’s biggest warehouse landlord is still full steam ahead on data centers. “After several quarters of sustained demand, we believe the market is entering its next phase,” Prologis CEO Dan Letter said in a second-quarter earnings call Thursday afternoon. The real estate investment trust (REIT) beat expectations in the second quarter of 2026, with record leasing and year-to-date data center starts already surpassing its full-year guidance. “The same land, customer relationships and operating capabilities that have made us the leader in logistics are enabling our data center and energy businesses, creating two additional long-term growth opportunities for Prologis,” Letter said during the call. The quarterly results come just six days before Prologis has a big decision to make. It must either make a formal offer to buy Segro, Britain’s biggest warehouse REIT and its international rival, or abandon its unsolicited bid. Segro’s board firmly rejected Prologis’ $16.6 billion all-share acquisition proposal in June. The company’s executives declined to take questions on the offer, but Letter told attendees that they’d put forward a “very compelling proposal.” “It offers a meaningful premium to where the stock has traded, and values the business above its stated [net tangible assets],” Letter said. “Importantly, it gives Segro shareholders the opportunity to participate in the upside of a stronger combined company, and the value of the Prologis enterprise and everything it offers should not be overlooked.” The news that the company leadership could share, however, was highly positive. Prologis reported core funds from operations (FFO) of $1.56 billion, up from $1.4 billion a year earlier and $1.44 billion last quarter. Net income reached $1.06 billion for the second quarter, nearly doubling from the $570 million reported in the same period of 2025, and up significantly from $980.5 million last quarter. Total revenue also grew annually, from $2.18 billion to $2.43 billi
bullets: Prologis beat Q2 2026 expectations; record leasing and data-center starts exceed full-year guidance.
CEO: 'market entering next phase' of sustained data center demand.
Prologis leveraging logistics land, customer relationships, operating capabilities for data center and energy.
stories.summary: Prologis, the world's largest warehouse REIT, beat Q2 2026 expectations with record leasing activity and data center starts already surpassing full-year guidance. CEO Dan Letter stated the market is 'entering its next phase' of data center growth, with Prologis leveraging its logistics platform, customer relationships, and operating capabilities to build two additional long-term growth vectors (data centers and energy). The strong results come amid a pending decision on Prologis' unsolicited $16.6B bid for UK rival Segro.
stories.impact: Industrial and data-center capital intensity will accelerate land and capital competition in logistics-heavy markets (TX, CA, AZ, GA). For multifamily developers, this signals tightening land availability in secondary markets near logistics/data-center clusters and potential upward pressure on land costs in Tier 2 metros; also indicates competing capital allocation toward industrial/DC over multifamily in some markets.

stories.title: Miami secures $193M permit to build civic center at Freedom Park
stories.excerpt: The city of Miami’s new civic center, which will replace an aging administrative building downtown and the city’s iconic city hall in Coconut Grove, secured a $193 million construction permit, the city confirmed. The city received its permit July 1,  just over a week before construction workers topped off the eight-story, 257,000-square-foot building on July 10. Adler Development is the developer, and Moss Construction is the general contractor.  The building will be part of the 130-acre Miami Freedom Park development, which is a $1 billion project anchored by Inter Miami CF’s stadium near Miami International Airport. The project, which includes […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Miami secured $193M permit for 257k-sf civic center at Freedom Park (130-acre, $1B mixed-use development).
Eight-story building topped off July 2026; completion spring 2028; $20M Metrorail pedestrian bridge.
Inter Miami CF stadium anchors broader development; consolidates city admin from two historic sites.
stories.summary: Miami's new civic center at the 130-acre, $1 billion Miami Freedom Park development secured a $193 million construction permit in July 2026. The 257,000-sq-ft, eight-story building (by Adler Development and Moss Construction) will consolidate city administrative functions from downtown and Coconut Grove City Hall, with a $20 million pedestrian bridge connecting to the Metrorail station. Completion is expected by spring 2028. Inter Miami CF's stadium anchors the broader mixed-use project near Miami International Airport.
stories.impact: Miami Freedom Park's anchor civic center and Metrorail connectivity will drive significant employment and transit ridership near the 73-acre civic site. For multifamily developers in South Florida, this validates Midtown Miami/Freedom Park submarket demand; suggests strong worker/service-employee housing demand around the civic center and stadium; indicates Metrorail accessibility premium and opportunity for transit-oriented multifamily near the pedestrian bridge connector.

stories.title: Santa Monica, elevated: Related sets new standard for SoCal luxury with lifestyle amenities, Thermador appliances
stories.excerpt: Today’s luxury buyer isn’t just looking for fancy finishes and great views; they’re expecting a residence that facilitates their lifestyle. In our latest Developer Diary, TRD visited 700 Broadway and sat down with Related California’s Peter Garofalo to learn how the developer created an exclusive luxury residential experience in one of SoCal’s most desirable submarkets. “The ideal resident for 700 Broadway is someone who wants to have it all in a wonderfully designed and luxurious setting,” said Garofalo. “Every space has been perfectly designed to keep a balance between the beachside ease and the city’s bustling energy.” With nearly 70,000 […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Related's 700 Broadway in Santa Monica: three-building luxury (nearly 70k sf amenity space).
Private 3/4-acre courtyard, rooftop pools/hot tubs/sauna, Speakeasy bar, indoor recreation resort model.
Nature-centric, handmade-materials design; beachside ease + urban energy positioning.
stories.summary: Related California's 700 Broadway in Santa Monica represents a new luxury residential standard for Southern California. The three-building development (brick and glass facades) encompasses nearly 70,000 square feet of curated amenity space, including a private three-quarter-acre courtyard, rooftop resort with dual pools, hot tubs, sauna barrels, and BBQ areas, and an indoor Speakeasy-style bar with recreational facilities. Design philosophy emphasizes natural/handmade materials blending beachside ease with urban energy.
stories.impact: 700 Broadway's ultra-high-amenity resort model and premium positioning set new market expectations for luxury multifamily in high-barrier coastal markets. For developers in LA/SoCal, this signals elevated resident expectations around experiential amenities, outdoor space, and design quality; likely pressures achievable rents in competing luxury projects and sets tone for capital requirements for Class A developments in the submarket.

stories.title: Moratorium on waterfront project applications in downtown West Palm heads to final vote
stories.excerpt: West Palm Beach commissioners will take a final vote on pausing new project applications for high-rise waterfront developments at next week’s meeting.  The proposed ordinance would create a six-month moratorium on new planned development applications for properties south of Monroe Drive, north of Southern Boulevard, west of Flagler Drive and east of Washington Road, according to the commission agenda. Commissioners approved it on first reading at a meeting last week; a second reading vote is scheduled for July 20.  The city cited rapid transformation of that corridor of apartment and condo buildings and is hiring Zyscovich to complete a zoning […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: West Palm Beach imposing six-month moratorium on waterfront high-rise applications (south Monroe to Southern Blvd).
Pause effective until Jan. 20, 2027; city hiring Zyscovich for zoning/economic analysis and new regulations.
More than a dozen luxury condo projects in works (2,000+ units); Related, Terra, BH Group active.
stories.summary: West Palm Beach commissioners approved (first reading) a six-month moratorium on new planned development applications for high-rise waterfront projects in downtown (south of Monroe Drive to Southern Boulevard). The pause, set to expire January 20, 2027, is a 'direct response' to overdevelopment concerns. The city has hired Zyscovich to conduct zoning/economic analysis and draft new regulations. More than a dozen luxury condo projects totaling 2,000+ units are currently underway, led by Miami-based developers including Related Group, Terra, and BH Group.
stories.impact: West Palm Beach moratorium signals regulatory pushback on waterfront condo density after rapid market acceleration. For multifamily developers: creates six-month window for projects already in pipeline to secure approvals; signals potential zoning restrictions post-moratorium (lower heights, density caps); indicates condo market may face supply constraints, potentially supporting multifamily rents in the submarket as condo buyers shift to rental demand.

stories.title: MidPen floats 110-unit affordable housing project on former Wells Fargo site in Sunnyvale
stories.excerpt: Developer interest in downtown Sunnyvale continues with another multifamily project proposal as more tech and artificial intelligence workers flock to the Silicon Valley city.  Foster City-based nonprofit MidPen Housing is planning a 110-unit affordable housing building at 295 South Mathilda Avenue in Sunnyvale, the East Bay Times reported. All of the units, save for a market-rate manager’s unit, will be income restricted.  The development was proposed at a former Wells Fargo bank site, which would give way to the residential building. The development earned support from some residents and community groups who claim it “will assist the city by better […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: MidPen Housing proposing 110-unit affordable project at former Wells Fargo site, downtown Sunnyvale.
25% of units designated for previously unhoused individuals; all units income-restricted except mgr unit.
AI/tech employment surge driving Sunnyvale multifamily interest; Beam Reach proposing 265-unit office-to-residential conversion.
stories.summary: Foster City-based nonprofit MidPen Housing is planning a 110-unit affordable housing building at 295 South Mathilda Avenue in downtown Sunnyvale, converting a former Wells Fargo bank site. All units except a manager unit will be income-restricted, with 25% designated for previously unhoused residents. The project earned support from community groups emphasizing smart, high-density housing utilization. Concurrently, Dallas-based Beam Reach is proposing a 265-unit residential conversion of former Google offices, reflecting surge in Sunnyvale multifamily demand driven by AI/tech employment decentralization from San Francisco.
stories.impact: Sunnyvale emerging as multifamily growth node as AI/tech employment bleeds out of San Francisco to Silicon Valley. For developers: Sunnyvale land and conversion economics improving sharply; regulatory support for high-density housing; workforce demand from tech/AI sectors supporting strong rents; expect competition for sites and rising land costs; favorable window for office-to-residential conversions and new construction before supply tightens.

stories.title: Pair of Frisco entrepreneurs ordered to pay $400M in damages to Georgetown business over alleged fraud scheme
stories.excerpt: Frisco entrepreneurs Gopala Krishnan and Sakthivel Gounder were ordered to pay $400 million in damages to a Georgetown-based business after being found liable for breach of contract and fraud by a Williamson County jury.  The pair concocted promotional materials for a luxury development called “The District” that they claimed was going to be built around the San Gabriel River. Krishnan and Gounder were sold the land by the company that sued them, Energy Commissioning Incorporated, owned by Marshall Hussain.  Energy Commissioning was subsequently hired by the pair for $21 million to build necessary infrastructure for a 400-acre, luxury waterfront development […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Frisco developers Gopala Krishnan and Sakthivel Gounder ordered to pay $400M for fraud on 'The District'.
Scheme: false luxury waterfront development (Georgetown); $21M contract for fake infrastructure; $5B bond never raised.
Prior SEC enforcement (2023) for alleged Ponzi scheme; developers swindled owner out of his home.
stories.summary: Frisco entrepreneurs Gopala Krishnan and Sakthivel Gounder were ordered by a Williamson County jury to pay $400 million in damages for defrauding Georgetown business owner Marshall Hussain. The pair created promotional materials for a fake 400-acre luxury waterfront development called 'The District' around the San Gabriel River, contracted Hussain's Energy Commissioning for $21 million in infrastructure work that never materialized, and pitched European bankers on an inflated plan while making only symbolic payments. The pair never raised a $5 billion bond. Both had been targeted by the SEC in 2023 for alleged Ponzi scheme activity.
stories.impact: Minimal direct multifamily relevance; case illustrates broader real estate fraud ecosystem and reputational risk in master-planned development. Developers should monitor SEC enforcement and litigation trends for warning signals on fraudulent schemes; case underscores capital-raising risks and lender/investor due diligence requirements in large mixed-use/master-planned development models.

stories.title: Bellhops and bass fishing: Wildhaven developer bets big on nature-centric luxury in Denton
stories.excerpt: A planned luxury resort might be coming to Denton, complete with six miles of horse trails to help bring residents and visitors closer to nature.  Sarasota, Florida-based An Ki Land Development’s managing director, Logan Breen, told the Dallas Business Journal that the new resort they plan on building is intended to combine upscale homes and hotels with “authentic outdoor experiences,” with a total value between $350 and $450 million when completed. The resort, called Wildhaven, is set to span 740 acres on the north side of Denton. 152 homes are set to be built in the resort, ranging from $1.5 […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: An Ki Land's Wildhaven: 740-acre luxury resort in north Denton; 152 homes ($1.5M–$3M+), 100-key hotel.
Amenities: 6 miles horse trails, 10-acre bass fishing pond, spa, equestrian areas, restaurants; $350–450M total value.
400 acres developed, 300+ preserved for natural feel; Noble Classic Homes building Phase 1 (20 lots).
stories.summary: Sarasota-based An Ki Land Development is planning Wildhaven, a 740-acre luxury resort in north Denton combining upscale residential and hospitality with outdoor experiences. The project includes 152 homes priced $1.5 million to $3 million+ (1-acre+ lots), a 100-key hotel, spa, equestrian facilities, six miles of horse trails, and a 10-acre bass fishing pond. Total value: $350–450 million. Noble Classic Homes is building Phase 1 (20 lots); design firm HKS handling masterplan. 400 acres will be developed; 300+ preserved for nature preservation.
stories.impact: Wildhaven represents high-amenity, nature-centric resort residential development model targeting affluent buyers seeking experiential/lifestyle communities. For Denton-area multifamily developers: signals competitive pressure from luxury resort and master-planned models in the submarket; indicates strong demand for premium residential near Dallas; suggests Denton's rapid growth (cited by developer) supports mixed product types; limited direct multifamily impact unless An Ki's resort model displaces traditional apartment demand.
